scenarios.title: stETH/ETH June 2022 depeg replay
scenarios.description: Historical reconstruction of the June 2022 stETH discount widening against ETH. ETH daily closes are archived closes; stETH uses published depeg anchors interpolated into a daily replay window so the oracle can be evaluated against a real market event instead of a synthetic shock.

sources.label: stETH traded near 0.95 ETH in mid-June 2022

scenarios.tagline: Real contagion, real forced deleveraging, real historical price path.
highlights: Real stETH/ETH June 2022 event data instead of a hand-drawn stress curve.
Shows how static 80% lending would have walked straight into default risk.
PegShield tightens early enough to keep loss prevention visible in dollars.

scenarios.title: mSOL/SOL liquidity vacuum
scenarios.description: Depth disappears in a few intervals, the peg gaps lower, and recovery never fully repairs the damage.
scenarios.tagline: Fast gap down, shallow rebound, toxic liquidation window.
highlights: Peg stress outruns liquidity before liquidators can recycle capital.
Oracle tightening matters most during the violent first leg, not after the rebound.
Peak prevented loss happens before the path has time to stabilize.

scenarios.title: mSOL/SOL reflexive bank run
scenarios.description: The market sells in waves: first panic, brief relief, then a second leg lower as confidence breaks.
scenarios.tagline: Two-leg selloff with a fake recovery in the middle.
highlights: The mid-event bounce is exactly where static policy creates false confidence.
A second stress leg keeps the regime detector under pressure longer.
Time spent in CRITICAL matters here almost as much as the peak drawdown.

scenarios.title: mSOL/SOL slow grind depeg
scenarios.description: No single violent move — the peg drifts a little deeper every interval for days until the cumulative dislocation is dangerous.
scenarios.tagline: Death by a thousand cuts; no single candle tells the story.
highlights: Tests whether the oracle catches slow impairment before any single day looks alarming.
Static 80% policy looks fine on any given day, but bleeds loss continuously.
Critical regime trips late here — detection latency is the risk being measured.

scenarios.title: mSOL/SOL single-wick false alarm
scenarios.description: An isolated wick prints an ugly -2% peg deviation for one interval, then snaps cleanly back to baseline.
scenarios.tagline: Was that a real depeg or a bad print? The oracle has to decide.
highlights: A risk oracle that panics on every wick is useless for production lending.
This path probes false-positive behavior: does CRITICAL clear once the signal reverts?
Any sustained LTV cut after the wick recovers is a regime-detector failure.

scenarios.title: mSOL/SOL flash crash repricing
scenarios.description: The peg breaks violently, prints an ugly wick, then mean-reverts faster than the worst-case scenarios.
scenarios.tagline: Short, brutal dislocation with a fast snapback.
highlights: A good risk oracle should survive false positives without staying punitive forever.
This path tests whether the system can tighten hard and still exit CRITICAL cleanly.
Fast mean reversion is visible in both theta stabilization and the loss-prevented curve.

scenarios.title: mSOL/SOL weekend redemption panic
scenarios.description: A confidence shock triggers rushed exits into thin weekend liquidity, then partially recovers once redemptions reopen.
scenarios.tagline: Confidence shock first, balance-sheet clarity later.
highlights: Designed to mimic a weekend confidence spiral where liquidity vanishes before fundamentals settle.
Static policy keeps lending into the worst part of the panic because the recovery comes too late.
PegShield only needs a few intervals of tightening to materially cut the bad-debt window.

scenarios.title: mSOL/SOL redemption spiral
scenarios.description: A collateral-side discount feeds a wider market selloff, then the peg stabilizes only after forced deleveraging has already propagated.
scenarios.tagline: Discount begets liquidations, liquidations beget more discount.
highlights: Targets the exact feedback loop a collateral risk oracle is supposed to break.
Peak loss prevented rises during the cascading leg, not after the market finally settles.
Useful for demoing that the oracle reacts to path-dependence, not just terminal drawdown.

scenarios.title: mSOL/SOL bridge contagion unwind
scenarios.description: Cross-venue stress leaks into the peg, correlation spikes, and a restaking narrative unwind keeps the asset impaired longer than usual.
scenarios.tagline: Contagion spreads across venues before the peg can normalize.
highlights: Models slower healing even after the initial liquidity event has passed.
Shows why a risk oracle needs persistence, not just a fast twitch response.
The recovery is real, but not fast enough to justify restoring fixed-policy leverage immediately.
